data.title: Wall Street Roundup: Nvidia Dips, Bitcoin Cracks, Retail Winners Emerge
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Nvidia earnings - strong results driven by data center growth, but shares slip as markets turn cautious (0:25). Bitcoin drops below $83K, crypto weakness spreads (1:10). Retail earnings: Home Depot vs. Lowe’s; Walmart vs. Target (1:50). Fintech & BNPL pressure (4:20). September jobs data: negative revisions revive recession worries; unemployment rises (5:45). Tariff rollbacks and mixed Fed signals swing December rate-cut odds sharply (7:15). Thanksgiving week preview (9:20).

Transcript

Rena Sherbill: Brian Stewart, our Director of news at Seeking Alpha. Welcome back to another week of Wall Street Roundup.

Brian Stewart: Great to be here, thank you.

Rena Sherbill: Great to have you. Talk to us about this week. What's top of mind for you?

Brian Stewart: Yeah, obviously, Nvidia (NVDA) earnings were the centerpiece. In terms of results, the company remains indomitable, 66% data center growth driving strong revenue growth, better than expected results.

However, the market is squeamish. Initially, you saw gains in Nvidia, but eventually finished lower in the day after its earnings release. I think that points to an overall just concern about the economic situation, the prospects of an AI bubble and just a move away from more speculative investments.

I think people are trying to rein in their vision of when certain investments might pay off. They're bringing in their time horizon a little bit. And I think that plays out especially in the Bitcoin (BTC-USD) market.

You see Bitcoin continuing to slide below $83,000 as we started this call. It's the first time since April it's lost about a third of its value from its high. You can see that play out in a lot of the Bitcoin related stocks.

You see (COIN) down 14%, Strategy (MSTR) down 16%, Circle (CRCL) down 16%, Robinhood (HOOD) down 12%. This is all over the last five days. So I think that's a clear sign that people are continuing to reconfigure their portfolios for a little bit more safety.

Rena Sherbill: And earnings wise, what else did you see?

Brian Stewart: Moving beyond Nvidia, it was a big week for retailers and a big week for pairs of retailers and the different reactions to them.

For example, Home Depot (HD) dropped about six percent after its earnings. It missed on the bottom line. It cut its guidance. It's got a cautious outlook due to the prospects for consumer spending, a soft housing market.

So in general, some signs that there's hope maybe in 2026 that the housing market will bounce back. That's really dependent on how the economy shapes up. So people are starting to build in some doubts about about that aspect.

Meanwhile, you saw Lowe's (LOW). It dropped in sympathy with Home Depot when Home Depot's earnings came out, but then bounced when its own earnings were released. It beat expectations better than expected guidance. So the guidance still showed some caution about the same kind of factors that Home Depot is looking for.

Also, Lowe's have been down about 20 % since September going into its earnings, so that bounce, but you do see the two stocks, the different kind of reaction to the earnings coming out. And I think it's just a play on Lowe's maybe executing a little better in a tough environment than Home Depot has been.

Turning to another set of paired stocks, you can see Walmart (WMT) was up 6% on its earnings. It's not far from its 52 week high. It's within five or 10% of its 52 week high at this point. It topped expectations thanks to strong e-commerce, strong international sales. So you see Walmart, people turning to value, you see strong execution from them.

On the other side of the coin, you see Target (TGT), which dropped after its earnings. Target had its third consecutive quarter of declining comparable store sales. Comparable store sales were down about 2.7% for the most recent quarter.

And so again, I think you're seeing an execution differential there. You're seeing Walmart execute much better. But I think you also see a difference in business plan. You see Walmart having a more of a value play, having more of its business focused on things like groceries and essentials. So you're seeing, I think, consumers step down in terms of the costs they're willing to absorb.

Then I think you're also just seeing Walmart benefiting from the scale that it has. I think even in the tariff situation that we're in, the uncertainty, I just think a company the size of Walmart has more leverage to keep things steady, even as the environment is a little bit shaky.

Rena Sherbill: And speaking to that environment, what are you seeing broadly speaking, economically?

Brian Stewart: So one interesting sector to look at is the buy now pay later fintech sector. Financial stocks that are more in sort of the speculative economy. Klarna (KLAR) reported earnings this past week. It's the first report since it came public. It dropped 9 % after earnings, continued to fall after that, now down about 16 % for the last five days.

It posted a loss and I think most damning, especially for the sector in general, the provision for credit losses climbed to 235 million. This is compared to 116 million a year ago.

So you're seeing companies start to show concern about delinquencies and consumers not being able to pay back. You see Affirm (AFRM), which is a competitor of Klarna, down 13 % in last five days.

And then outside of that particular sector, you also see companies like SoFi (SOFI) is down 10 % in last five days, PayPal (PYPL) is down 7%. So I think there's some concern that if consumer cracks, the first place we're going to see that happen is in these financial stocks that are based on consumer lending or interactions with the consumer, small businesses, things like that.

And so I think just as we're looking ahead, especially as economic data is starting to come back, on the horizon, I think that's a key place to look.

Rena Sherbill: And what is coming out of that economic data that we're seeing, that we're getting these drips and drabs of previous economic data releases that are just now coming out? What are you seeing there?

Brian Stewart: Yeah, so this week we saw the jobs data. This is the jobs data for September. it's a backward looking report, but it beat expectations on the headline number. So we added 119,000 jobs in September, much better than expected.

However, the unemployment rate ticked up to 4.4%. This is largely due to people re-entering the job market. I think the most concerning aspect of the report was the revisions for previous months. So August is now showing a loss of 4,000 jobs, whereas before it was showing a gain of 22,000 jobs.

When you see the number cross that zero mark, so we actually had the hint of a recession in August with those job losses. Now, that could just be a one-time blip. It could come back, but it had been an extremely weak set of jobs report even going into the September report.

So September being better than expected really didn't do much to soothe concerns about the economy, especially since you're seeing a pattern of relatively strong reports and then revisions later. So if we see the September number get revised lower in the future, I think that'll be a meaningful step, especially as we wait for the backlog of economic data that's coming out.

Rena Sherbill: And we're seeing a rollback of some tariffs in the past couple of days. How does that play into this economic conversation or is that an obvious cause and effect? Like if people aren't doing that well, they're rolling back some tariffs.

Brian Stewart: Yeah. I think, it's coming at a good time, obviously. mean, to your point, it's not confusing why it's happening now. And, I mean, one thing, however you feel about the Trump administration, it is responsive to different political winds. So it'll throw something out, try it. If it doesn't work, there's a lot of pushback. They'll pull back, they'll reassess, move around.

The tariffs is the best example of that. There hasn't been a consistent tariff policy for more than a couple of weeks the entire time the tariff discussion has been going on. You can see the administration responding to the calls for affordability and you can see that play out in the Fed as well.

There was high expectation that there'd be a series of rate cuts coming. And then as the economic data started to come in and people began worried about affordability, became worried about inflation again, you see the Fed sort of pulling back.

And now we're in sort of a mixed situation with the Fed where expectations for what's going to happen in the December meeting are bouncing around a lot. So for instance, just in the last couple of days, yesterday, the market was pricing in a 40 % chance that the Fed would cut rates at the December meeting.

Today, it's not pricing in a 75% chance that that would happen. So it's actually flip-flopping in terms of probably won't happen, probably will happen. And the main news today is that the New York Fed President said there could be some room for further rate cuts. He's a pretty heavy voice among policymakers, and so people are putting a lot of weight on that.

However, if you look at the other comments that have come from Fed speakers in recent weeks, it's pretty mixed. Some think no cuts, some think there should be a cut. The minutes of the last Fed meeting show that there was a pretty sharp division, pretty hot debate among the policymakers of whether or not there should be a rate cut. So I really think you're going to see that number, the odds of a rate cut December flip-flop a lot between now and when the meeting takes place. I think it's going to be difficult to get any clarity on that.

Rena Sherbill: What else would you add to this week's conversation?

Brian Stewart: Looking forward, we have Thanksgiving next week, so the market is going to be closed on Thursday, half day Friday, there's going to be less volume just because people are out of the office. So you could see volatility increase in a condition like that.

So if some unexpected good or bad news comes out, you can see a little more dramatic move. And then next week is very, because of the holiday, all the news releases really pumped into the first three days. You're getting a bunch of September economic data that had been halted because of the government shutdown. You're gonna get a flood of that next week.

Plus there's a bunch of earnings. mean, nothing really dramatic on the earnings schedule, just a lot of, I'm trying to be polite, second tier companies. You have companies like Deere (DE), you have another set of retailers coming out, you have Dick's (DKS), Best Buy (BBY), Abercrombie and Fitch (ANF).

And so you're going to get a fair amount of information, especially in the first three days, and then you're going to have lower volume. I just think it sets the stage and the market's already jittery. And so it sets the stage for a situation where I think the market's likely going to stay in a wait and see kind of attitude. So expect it to bounce around and feel things out for whatever reason.

Rena Sherbill: Yeah, patience is certainly key right now, maybe more than ever. It's certainly key right now in terms of the upcoming week and we'll be off next week. So we will talk to you again in a couple of weeks, but any sense of Black Friday and what that may portend?

Brian Stewart: Yeah, that's a good point, especially on the subject of retailers. I think we're going to get some data, we're going to hear the data about that the week after. So I do think it's a crucial data point as we go into the holiday season, especially on two fronts. Retailers specifically, how well they're going to close out the year when you're looking at the Target, Walmart divide in retailers where it's really been a stock pickers market in retailers lately.

And then the second aspect of Black Friday is just the overall economic information that we're going to get from it. So how is the consumer doing? Are they spending? Are they trying to find deals?

Are they trying to move up to luxury brands just because, maybe in an inflationary environment where mid-tier things are more expensive than you expect, maybe there's some value in just going whole hog and buying luxury items, things of that nature. So we're gonna get some information.

Rena Sherbill: Well, happy Thanksgiving to everybody. Happy Thanksgiving, Brian. Gratitude is a great attitude to have, to keep in mind. Talk to you soon.

Brian Stewart: Talk to you soon.

data.title: Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Week 246: $24,600 Allocated, $2,737.06 In Projected Dividends
data.description:
This was a difficult week as the markets finished negative on fears of an A.I. bubble. The crypto markets, which so many people had argued weren’t correlated to the economy, have also been crashing as more investors are getting scared of another crypto winter. The labor market is also looking a bit week as additional layoffs have occurred and its starting to look as if the Fed is going to use the lack of data as a reason not to cut rates. CME Group now has the chance of a rate cut at 32.8% for the upcoming meeting in December. All eyes are on Nvidia (NVDA) as Jensen Huang is becoming the last hope to unravel the bear thesis about A.I. being a bubble. I'm excited because all the data is pointing to a blowout quarter. Big tech has discussed how large their backlogs are for compute, and their allocations toward capex continue to grow. There's still time for an end of the year rally as strong guidance and earnings from NVDA can restore confidence in the bulls and have fresh capital come in to buy the dip. I'm exited because I think the retracement is an opportunity for long-term investors, and eventually we will have a new Fed chair with a much different outlook on where rates should be.

The markets are still choppy, and I believe there have been many long-term opportunities forming. After allocating $24,600 to the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio, the account balance finished week 246 at $31,761.61. The account's profitability has decreased (-7.88%) to $7,161.61, which is a return on invested capital of 29.62%. This week I finally added the Goldman Sachs Nasdaq 100 Premium Income ETF (GPIQ) to the portfolio and increased my position in Blue Owl Capital Corporation (OBDC). The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio generated $36.76 of dividend income in week 246. The combination of reinvesting the generated dividend income and making these investments increased the forward projected annualized dividend income by $14.87 (0.55%) to $2,737.06. The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio has generated $5,241.91 of income since its inception, which is 21.31% of the total capital I have allocated toward the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio. I have a feeling that NVDA is going to blow earnings estimates out of the water, and we will see a strong reaction from the market next week that will ignite an end of the year rally in equities.

The Overall Performance of the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio

The markets have been choppy, and the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio lost a bit of ground this week. I took the opportunity to add GPIQ on a pullback, which I believe will be a solid position going forward. I'm now generating $2,737.06 in forward dividend income which is a portfolio yield of 8.62% and a yield on cost of 11.13%. I'm currently up 31.73%, or $7,774.44, on invested capital. When I think about starting this account with $100, and adding $100 per week toward income producing assets, I'm quite pleased with the progress. My focus was to build a portfolio that would mitigate risk while generating recurring income. I wanted capital appreciation to be a secondary goal, as I was not focused on replicating or trying to exceed the market's return. I am excited to see where this goes, as I think there's a good chance I will be generating around $2,850 in forward income heading into 2026.

The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Dividend Section

Here's how much dividend income is generated per investment basket:

  • ETFs $986.56 (36.05%)
  • Equities $605.17 (22.12%)
  • REITs $443.23 (16.20%)
  • REITs $436.76 (15.96%)
  • BDCs $264.73 (9.67%)

Investors utilize dividends to supplement their income and live off them. I'm building a dividend portfolio for myself 30 years into the future. In 2022, I collected $507.80 in dividend income from 533 dividends. In 2023, I collected $978.11 in dividend income from 660 dividends. 2024 was a great year as the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio generated $1,623.14 in dividend income from 709 dividends. After 46 weeks in 2025, $2,062.23 of dividend income was produced, which is 127.05% of the total income from 2024. There were 682 positions that paid a dividend, which is 96.19% of the dividends generated in 2024.

There was $36.76 of dividend income generated this week, which brought the average amount of dividend income I have generated on a weekly basis to $44.83. This is an increase of $13.62 (43.62%) YoY, and there have been four weeks that exceeded $100, unlike in 2024, when the largest week was $89.13. I'm really excited for the rest of October to unfold as the YoY data is showing me a huge trend going up and to the right with dividend income. Over the next several years, I should be able to exceed $5,000 in forward dividend income in this account and basically double my annualized allocation to the portfolio.

October ended up breaking my previous record of $209.73 in dividend income, as I generated $229.93. November is going well as I have generated $91.76 in dividend income so far. I just looked at the calendar, and because of when Thanksgiving falls, I'm not sure what the last week in November will look like. When I look at the chart below, December was the second highest month last year and I have a feeling that I'm going to exceed $225 in both November and December this year. The year is winding down, and I really like the way the YoY progress looks on a monthly basis, and I'm excited for 2026, as I think there's a lot of opportunities in the market for income producing equities.

My long-term goal is to have 75% or more of the portfolio generate at least one share per year from their dividend income. There are currently 43 positions accomplishing this, which is emphasized by the green section of the table below. There's $499.31 of forward dividend income being generated from the new dividends being generated from all the positions generating at least one share from their dividends annually. Many positions are still lagging, but I'm making a strong effort to allocate capital toward them so I can get at least 50% of the portfolio to accomplish this goal by the end of the year. My fixation on this is because it will allow me to advance the compounding impacts from the dividend snowball, and it also makes sure that I am not investing in the same positions each week.

The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Composition

ETFs are exceeding 20% of the portfolio again, and it's entirely my fault. I was also adding to REITs heading into the rate cut, which caused the portfolio to get a bit top-heavy. It's difficult for me to stay away from these sectors, especially after big tech crushed earnings. I'm going to work on evening out the sectors and having them more proportional in the future. Individual equities now represent 39.58% of the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio while generating 22.12% of the dividend income. REITs, ETFs, CEFs, and BDCs make up 60.42% of the portfolio and generate 77.88% of the forward income. I'm working on getting individual equities to represent more of the portfolio. The long-term goal is to have the sectors be close to equal weight, but that may not happen for 5-10 years at this rate, if ever. I have been adding energy companies as I believe there is a big opportunity there, and I will probably continue allocating capital toward energy positions as time progresses.

Altria Group (MO) started to climb again and represents 4.12% of the portfolio. At times like this it goes to show how important diversification is considering MO helped offset my losses this week. I'm happy with the way things are going and I really wouldn’t feel bad adding to any of these companies except for MO here. I'm expecting the top 20 positions to change a bit next year, and I am hoping that I can get all the positions a bit more even in the future. While I have allowed myself a 5% maximum, I really want to have a well-rounded portfolio that can mitigate risk well.

None of the top-10 positions changed this week but some did pay dividends, and I added to STWD. My allocation to the top 10 positions is $6,858.76, and they finished the week with a value of $8,959.01. This is an ROI of $2,100.24 (30.62%). There has been $1,432.60 of dividends generated and reinvested, which is 20.89% of the initial investment into these positions. I'm now projecting that $742.24 of dividend income will be generated from the top 10 holdings, which have a forward yield of 10.82%. These positions represent 28.17% of the portfolio, while their forward dividend income is projected to generate 27.12% of the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio's annualized income.

Week 246 Additions

This week I added to Goldman Sachs Nasdaq 100 Premium Income ETF (GPIQ) to the portfolio and increased my position in Blue Owl Capital Corporation (OBDC).

Goldman Sachs Nasdaq 100 Premium Income ETF

  • GPIQ’s dynamic covered call strategy leaves upside partially uncapped, enabling strong monthly income and market-beating total returns compared to peer ETFs.
  • GPIQ offers a compelling blend of capital appreciation and double-digit yield, amassing $2.12B AUM in just over two years
  • I like GPIQ’s focus of generating recurring monthly income while also producing capital appreciation.
  • GPIQ can only write call options between 25% and 75% of the value of the equity investments, which leaves a portion of the portfolio always uncapped, so no matter what ratio they choose, GPIQ will appreciate in a bullish environment.

Blue Owl Capital Corporation

  • I bought more OBDC because it remains a compelling income investment, trading at a -19.6% discount to NAV with a robust dividend yield.
  • Even with potential dividend adjustments in a lower-rate environment, OBDC's yield remains attractive, and I remain bullish, adding to my position.
  • I think that a lower rate environment will be good for BDCs such as OBDC because their origination volume should expand and they could replace the depleted earnings through volume.

Week 247 Gameplan

I am considering adding to Roundhill Innovation-100 0DTE Covered Call Strategy ETF (QDTE) next week.

Conclusion

This wasn’t the best week, but the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio is still doing well. I added GPIQ to the portfolio, and the forward income being produced is exceeding $2,700. The S-curve is forming, and I'm very excited about the progress that has been made. I started looking through the portfolio, and I'm considering doing a RIF at the end of the year and exiting the bottom 5-10% of positions and redeploying that capital. I may actually do that on an annual basis. Now that the portfolio has over 100 positions, I may need to put that rule in effect. I'm still focused on building out the stream of income, and I can’t wait until I'm reinvesting more than 100% of my monthly allocations from the dividend income being produced. Hopefully NVDA crushes earnings and puts the bear case about A.I. being a bubble to bed. Please leave all of your comments and suggestions below, as I enjoy interacting with everyone.

data.title: LendingClub: Taking The Next Step On Its Growth Journey
data.description:
The digital bank LendingClub (LC), which has long specialized in refinancing consumer credit card debt, has had a busy few months.

I last wrote about LC in August, following the company's release of its second-quarter earnings results. Since then, the company has reported strong third-quarter results, continuing to generate higher loan originations while executing its product road map. A few weeks later, management held an Investor Day and announced exciting new growth targets, along with some changes to the business model. The stock briefly surpassed $19 per share following these events, its highest level in over three years.

I continue to view LC as a buy. A significant accounting change starting next year is likely to appeal to more institutional investors, and higher medium-term growth targets should drive significantly higher returns over the next three to five years.

Business as usual in 3Q25

LC continued to grow earnings in the third quarter, delivering diluted earnings per share of $0.37, more than triple the company’s earnings a year ago. Revenue of $266 million grew 32% year over year, primarily driven by over $2.6 billion in loan originations. Both EPS and revenue beat Wall Street estimates.

Loan pricing improved in the quarter as interest rates continued to decline, and LC continues to ramp up its investment-grade structured certificate program, which it recently began selling to insurance companies.

One disappointment in the quarter was slower bank activity than I had expected. Due to their lower deposit funding sources, whole loan sales to banks generate the best sales prices. Whole loan sales of $550 million in the quarter have been ~flat for four quarters now, and sales to banks out of LC’s extended seasoning portfolio have not picked up in recent quarters either. (For more information about LC’s loan distribution channels, check out my previous article.)

The good news is that LC is still posting strong results, expanding its marketing efforts, and guiding for similar results in the current quarter, despite the fourth quarter typically being a seasonally weaker period for the company.

Taking the next step

The big surprise came a few weeks after third-quarter results when LC held an Investor Day for Wall Street analysts. The event featured several big announcements:

  • LC announced a $100 million share repurchase program, its first since becoming a bank.
  • Management provided strong medium-term growth targets, including strong origination growth and return on tangible common equity (ROTCE) targets.
  • The company announced new loan origination channels.
  • Management announced a significant accounting change that will take effect in 1Q26.

The share repurchase announcement is straightforward. The company has significant excess capital, and analysts and investors have been wondering when they might see an announcement for several quarters now.

As for growth, LC just achieved a $10 billion loan origination run rate in 3Q25. Over the medium term, management’s goal is to reach $18 billion to $22 billion in originations. Management didn’t define the medium term, but I think four years is probably a good way to think about it, assuming the economy doesn’t experience a significant recession or a period of stagflation.

Interestingly, LC has started to grow some of its personal loan sub-verticals nicely. While the core use case will always be credit card debt consolidation, management now expects to generate 15% to 20% of originations in the medium term through purchase finance use cases, such as home improvement loans, as well as its existing secured auto and small-business lending verticals.

In tandem with its Investor Day, LC announced the expansion into major purchase finance lending through the acquisition of select talent and technology from Mosaic, a former fintech firm, and through a partnership with WiseTack. WiseTack operates a network of 40,000 contractor merchants and other partnerships that will launch LC’s foray into this sub-vertical of personal lending. The company will begin purchasing participation certificates from WiseTack in early 2026 and then transition to its own underwriting model in the middle of next year, allowing it to originate larger loans that are not currently available through WiseTack.

While doubling originations may seem like a lofty goal, I believe it is certainly achievable, and my prior model already assumed about 25% loan origination growth in 2026. LC still has marketing channels it hasn’t fully utilized, and LC's addressable market is massive, considering the $1.3 trillion of outstanding consumer revolving debt in the U.S. LC has really never had an issue finding origination volume; the difficult part is finding capacity for where to place all of these loans.

Assuming management's growth expectations over the medium term, LC expects to significantly improve its ROTCE performance from approximately 12% to 13% currently to 18% to 20%. The expansion will come from balance sheet growth, net interest margin expansion, more loan sales at better prices, and operating leverage.

Moving to fair value accounting

Another major announcement at Investor Day is that LC will fully transition to fair value accounting for all newly originated loans starting in 1Q26. I encourage readers to review my past articles for a comprehensive explanation of loan accounting; however, I’ll provide a brief overview here, as it is a critical component of the business model.

When LC originates loans, it can sell them immediately to various types of loan investors, such as banks, or form structured certificates representing pools of loans, which include junior and senior tranches. Either the junior or senior tranches, or both together, can be sold to private credit or insurance companies. LC frequently sells the junior tranche and retains the senior tranche on its balance sheet, which is risk-remote in terms of losses.

LC accounts for loans retained on the balance sheet in different ways, including held-for-investment (HFI) and held-for-sale (HFS).

HFI loans are the traditional accounting for banks, in which they intend to hold the loan to maturity. However, because lending always includes some normalized level of losses, banks must reserve for HFI loans by taking a day-1 reserve for expected losses over the life of the vintage. For instance, LC took a $46 million provision in 3Q25 for new HFI originations, which immediately detracts from revenue and therefore earnings. Loans held on the balance sheet generate the highest returns, but over several years.

LC also maintains an extended seasoning portfolio of loans that it intends to hold for six to 12 months, collecting interest income in the process, before selling the loans to investors.

LC is not required to take a day-1 provision for these loans but must mark the portfolio to fair value each quarter. In 3Q25, LC had net fair value adjustments of -$38.4 million. These marks account for several adjustments, including the discount that LC may initially sell loans at, as well as the roll-down, which accounts for regular losses expected to occur over time on a vintage. However, the marks aren't all taken upfront and materialize over time, so they may appear less punitive.

Up until now, LC has typically classified anywhere from one-third to one-fifth of its quarterly originations as HFI. All new originations retained on the balance sheet will be classified as HFS starting in 2026. It’s a significant change, likely driven by the preferences of institutional investors who invest on shorter time horizons. Additionally, peers in the personal lending space that have elected HFS accounting have received stronger valuation multiples thus far.

Over time, whether using HFI or HFS accounting, CFO Drew LaBenne noted that the company will ultimately arrive at the same place.

Having to deal with both HFI and HFS likely became a headache for most investors, and the timing of revenue recognition is likely to result in higher earnings more quickly.

I, personally, liked the hybrid HFI-HFS loan accounting model because, in periods when the capital markets slowed down or froze and loan buyers moved to the sidelines, holding loans to maturity and taking the day-1 reserves upfront seemed like the best way to provide transparency and prepare for the unknown. However, I suspect that nothing is preventing LC from returning to HFI accounting if needed. I also continue to believe management uses an appropriate discount rate when marking its loans to fair value each quarter.

Q4 estimates and beyond

The current quarter will be business as usual for LC. I am projecting $0.39 diluted EPS, driven by slightly over $2.6 billion in originations.

Unfortunately, due to the significant accounting changes in 1Q26, I am not yet ready to project EPS estimates beyond the current quarter.

Projecting the fair value marks on the HFS book is one of the more complex modeling components of the company because there is so much that goes into this bucket.

But assuming the current accounting policies had remained intact, I would have projected approximately $1.68 of diluted EPS in 2026, which is $0.10 above consensus as of this writing.

Risks

The most considerable risk will continue to be the macro environment. LC’s model depends on a robust marketplace of loan buyers, who could get spooked by a severe recession that leads to credit deterioration or significantly higher interest rates that lead to a much higher cost of capital for loan buyers and therefore much higher return thresholds for investors, which can slow down loan volume.

In its Investor Day presentation, management assumes a stable interest rate environment, where the Federal Reserve lowers the federal funds rate close to 3%.

Whether that happens remains to be seen, but extreme volatility in the economy is likely to challenge LC, along with the rest of the sector, as experienced in the past.

Valuation

If I look at my model using LC’s current form of accounting, I have the company exiting next year somewhere near a 15% ROTCE. I also project the company to exit 2026 with a tangible book value of $14.13. Assuming a bank with a 15% ROTCE received a valuation of 1.5x TBV, I would value LC at $21.45 per share.

However, the old model doesn’t incorporate share repurchases, and I also suspect that earnings will look better under the new accounting change.

Where things get much more exciting is if LC successfully executes, doubling originations and achieving an 18% to 20% ROTCE.

Since the first quarter of 2021, when LC purchased Radius Bank and launched its new business model, the company has grown TBV per share by a compound annual growth rate (CAGR) of 14%.

If you subtract a one-time windfall in 2Q22 from the release of a valuation allowance against the company's deferred tax assets, I calculate an 11% CAGR. That's still quite good, considering the significant volatility over the past five years, stemming from the pandemic, rapidly rising interest rates, and the Silicon Valley Bank crisis. Consensus estimates, according to Visible Alpha, project LC’s TBV per share will exit this year around $12.27.

Assuming an 11% CAGR over the next four years, which I view as conservative, LC would grow TBV to $18.14 by the end of 2029. If it achieves a 20% ROTCE or is close to it, the stock should be valued at 2x TBV per share, resulting in a share price of $36.28, implying 127% upside from current levels.

Obviously, management has to execute, and the market won’t give LC this until there is clear evidence. The economy must also cooperate, but this demonstrates the strong potential of the stock at current levels.

data.title: Sandisk: A Little Correction Is Healthy
data.description:
Introduction

Sandisk Corporation (SNDK) saw a massive profit-taking action recently, prompting me to write an update since the last time I covered it back in September. Since then, the company’s share price went up by over 200% at one point, compared to no change in the SPY (SP500).

It seems that the recent drop has nothing to do with the company’s fundamentals, which are still improving, and more to do with market fears of a supposed AI bubble, which is a valid concern.

However, I don’t think this is the end of growth for SanDisk, and expect that after this healthy correction, the uptrend to resume. I am maintaining my Strong Buy rating.

By The Numbers

Starting from the top, sales topped $2.3B, up 21% y/y, and beat analysts’ estimates by around $160m. Looking at the revenue breakdown in more detail, we can see that Edge revenues are doing the heavy lifting here, just as expected, up 26% sequentially, with two hyperscalers in qualification, and the third planned for CY26, meaning two hyperscalers are already testing SNDK’s products to use in their data centers before making volume purchases or long-term commitments. Datacenter revenues are still down around 10% y/y, but a sequential increase of 26% is telling us that this downtrend is about to reverse. Consumer sales are up 27% y/y and 11% sequentially.

Looking at the company’s efficiency and profitability, Q1 non-GAAP EPS came in at $1.22, beating analysts’ estimates by a respectable 33 cents. Sequentially, we can see the company’s gross margins improved by 360bps, with operating margins drastically improving too from $18m to $176m, bringing its margin to 7.6% from less than 1% in Q4 ’25.

On an annual basis, we can see that even though there was a sizable improvement from last quarter, compared to Q1 ’25, margins across the board as still down quite a bit. Gross margins are still down 880bps, while operating margins are down around 1100bps compared to the same time last year. We can see that these are improving sequentially, and I expect this to continue.

Let’s look at the company’s financial position. SNDK finished the quarter with around $1.4B in cash and equivalents, against $1.3B in long-term debt, which was reduced by around $500m since the previous quarter. The management is paying down debt quite efficiently, which makes it a lot less risky right now, and with such an improved performance over the last quarter, the debt outstanding is not an issue, as it is easily covered by the cash on hand, and EBIT can easily cover interest expenses on the debt.

On top of these results, the management provided us with quite a few numbers for the next fiscal second quarter of 2026, as per below:

These numbers are mostly above what analysts expected. Revenue expectations were $2.3B-$2.36B, non-GAAP gross margin around 38%, while non-GAAP EPS in the range of $1.77-$2.08. So, analysts were expecting a mild improvement overall, but SNDK had other plans. Just a couple of days following these, what I would call quite impressive results, the company’s shares skyrocketed over 35% to reach around $285. The company has been on fire since separating from Western Digital (WDC).

Overall, as I said, the results show a decent turnaround is afoot, with the demand coming in from all the revenue segments, which will lead to further operational improvements, as long as the demand remains.

Why The Recent Drop?

Yesterday, November 20th, was a day full of ups and downs. Nvidia (NVDA) opened higher on the day due to still decent growth and expectations going forward, pushing the whole market into the green. The QQQ (QQQ) was up over 2% at one point, and everyone was cheering and dismissing the AI bubble fears once again. However, something shifted by midday as all the indices took a sharp turn down, erasing the 2% gain and ending the day 2% in the red. A whopping 4% move. It seems that the worst hit came to SanDisk, as the shares ended down 20% for the day, on seemingly no news whatsoever.

On that day, there was even an upgrade from Bank of America to SNDK’s target price, going from $270 to $300, which is just an unfortunate coincidence that the company’s stock plummeted this hard.

So, it seems that the fears of an AI bubble are alive and well. This has been quite a frothy market over the last couple of quarters. When my portfolio begins to look very good, it usually means the short-term top is in, and expect some sort of a correction. The company’s shares have been on fire, and many investors have begun to look a little worried right now, which is understandable. Might as well lock in some gains while there are some stills.

I don’t think this drop was based on the company’s fundamentals at all. The fundamentals look to be improving quite a bit. All the hyperscalers have reported their results over the last month, with NVDA being one of the last ones to report, and all of them are committing even more resources to keep advancing AI and the hype story forward. Meta (META) CEO Mark Zuckerberg, in interviews, mentioned that it is better to overspend than to fall behind when it comes to the possibility of achieving superintelligence much sooner, for example, 3 years from now instead of 5 or more, and that is understandable. This also means that all other CEOs involved in this AI race are thinking the same.

No one is going to back down right now, which means SNDK’s growth is not going to stop for at least a few more years. This will certainly lead to higher operational efficiency as fabs will be utilized close to full capacity as demand increases. Just as all the hyperscalers and the supporting semiconductor companies like TSMC (TSM) are increasing their Capex to meet the ever-growing demand for AI and data centers, so is SanDisk. SNDK and Kioxia announced that start if operations of the state-of-the-art semiconductor fabrication facility in Japan to meet the market demand for AI, called the Fab2.

These expenditures are not going to stop, and I expect to see further spending on new fabs going forward, since hyperscalers are going to go even harder than over the last year in terms of expanding capacity for data centers and power-hungry AI.

The company is developing new ultra-high-density eSSDs, supporting 5 hyperscaler collaborations, to power the AI and data center boom. These drivers leverage the new BiCS 8 QLC NAND “Stargate” controllers for better power efficiency, and we all know how much power is needed in the AI and data center business.

The company is looking to increase its R&D spending to 15% of total sales. To keep up in this hypergrowth era of AI, the company needs to continuously revamp its products, come out with more new iterations so that it retains its market share and even increases it if the product is the top product out there.

The question is, has all of this upside been priced in already? Looking at the company’s price action, it may seem so. The next quarter is going to be a lot more profitable, and even after such a run-up, the company’s forward P/E ratio is still at around 15, which is quite low if you ask me for such a company that is seeing top and bottom-line growing tremendously. With this new information available to us, let’s look at an updated valuation.

Valuation

Analysts are quite bullish regarding SNDK’s revenue and EPS growth. My assumptions are going to be a little bit more conservative, just so I get a larger margin of safety and account for the market jitters that we will no doubt going to experience over the next few months. Below are my assumptions.

Analysts expect the company’s sales to grow close to 40% for fiscal 2026, and even 21% next year. After that, the estimates are not trustworthy as it is hard to predict what is going to happen, so for my assumptions, I went with around 15% CAGR over the next 5 years, which, I think, is reasonable. Operating margins have been improving tremendously, and now, with the outlook for the next quarter looking a lot better than previously thought, analysts expect at least 12.6 in EPS for fiscal 2026, which grows by 50% the following year to 19, bringing the company’s forward P/E ratio to just 10. That is ridiculously low. I went with around 26% operating margins, which gives me the following EPS numbers. Much more achievable, in my opinion.

Additionally, the company’s WACC of 8% seemed to be too low and didn’t provide me with enough margin of safety, so I went with 10% as my discount rate for the calculations and kept 2.5% as my terminal growth rate. Now, even with these, what I consider quite achievable numbers, the company is still trading at a discount to its fair value.

Risks To The Thesis

Hyperscalers like Meta have been known to overspend and then aggressively cut back once it is too expensive. Just look at what happened in the past during the COVID pandemic: a massive hiring spree and subsequent laying off of thousands of employees. This will repeat; there’s no doubt about it. And when it does, expect the cyclicality to turn very negative, leading to underutilization of fabs, inefficiencies, and loss in profits. Companies like SNDK are very sensitive to cycles, and I expect this time is not going to be any different.

This sort of spending of billions and probably trillions of dollars over the next few years is surely unsustainable, and when that money dries out, be sure to rebalance your portfolio before it hits the fan.

Closing Comments

I would say there is still a good reason to own SNDK going forward. The money isn’t drying out just yet, so might as well take the opportunity while the momentum is still there. Just know the risks and know that it is not going to last forever. The recent drop is nothing to be afraid of. It is healthy to see such corrections. It also allows investors to get in at much more reasonable prices.

I could see further stock price declines given the AI bubble fears circling the markets, but I am certain this growth is going to continue.

data.title: Is Netflix Stock Binge-Worthy? Disney A Better Bid
data.description:
Why Is Netflix Stock Falling?

Netflix (NFLX) surged after its 10-for-1 stock split, with investors hoping the move would propel the stock’s valuation in a similar manner to its 2015 split. However, the stock has plummeted more than 14% in the last month, including nearly 5% in the last week amid news about its bid to acquire Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD). Paramount (PSKY) and former Alpha Picks stock Comcast (CMCSA) also reportedly have bids on WBD in an effort to gain access to Warner Bros.' massive film library and streaming business. Investors have raised concerns about strategic misalignment, antitrust challenges, and the potential impact on earnings. Netflix finds itself at a strategic crossroads — facing skepticism about altering a formula that works and pressure to act in a consolidating market.

NFLX Performance Since 2015 Stock Split

Paramount is eyeing a full buyout for $23.50 per share, but WBD is looking for a price closer to $30 per share. Netflix and Comcast are interested in WBD's streaming and studio operations, and Netflix told WBD management the streamer would keep releasing films in theaters if it acquired the studio.

Netflix would benefit from Warner Bros. Discovery’s deep content library, valuable franchises, and HBO’s prestige TV brand. But, compared to rival bidders, the streamer may have the smallest synergy opportunity and perhaps the toughest regulatory path to closing the deal, according to Morgan Stanley analyst Benjamin Swinburne. In the meantime, Swinburne said shares are under pressure over concerns an acquisition “would complicate the investment thesis, distract management, and/or dilute EPS.”

The market reaction comes despite Netflix’s ongoing pressure from investors to become more active on the dealmaking front, particularly as YouTube gains share. The company has historically focused on building rather than acquiring, a choice that has led to a strategic bind, JPMorgan analyst Doug Anmuth explained in a note:

Potential media industry consolidation has remained an overhang on (Netflix) shares, with concerns that a larger media company/studio could increase competition and potentially curb Netflix's ability to license third-party content, or that Netflix itself could get bogged down in M&A," Anmuth said.

Monthly TV Viewing Share by Platform and Streaming Breakdown (October 2025)

Despite the hype around the 10-for-1 stock split and potential strategic acquisition, Netflix’s underlying fundamentals and valuation profile essentially remained unchanged. Netflix trades at a steep premium relative to sector peers, which has kept its quant rating at a Hold. Another titan in the industry presents a better opportunity for investors, according to the SA Quant Rating system.

Netflix vs. Disney: Which Stock Is the Better Buy?

The Walt Disney Company (DIS) competes directly with Netflix for the attention of at-home viewers. Netflix does so as a pure-play streamer, while Disney competes through both its linear networks and its direct-to-consumer (DTC) streaming business — which includes Disney+ and Hulu. With a market cap of ~$183B and annual revenue of $94.42B, Disney is the No. 1 quant-rated Movies and Entertainment stock and among the top 10 in the Communications sector, showcasing a Strong Buy Quant Rating. Netflix has a market cap more than 10x larger than its annual revenue of $43.38B and a Hold rating.

Disney and Netflix are both profit-making machines, highlighted by strong operating margins, high returns on shareholder equity, along with robust cash flow generation. Netflix has better Growth and Momentum factor grades, while Disney has a clear edge in Valuation and EPS Revisions.

The following sections examine key metrics underlying the quant ratings. Seeking Alpha’s Quant Rating System captures over 100 metrics for each security, compares the metrics to those of sector peers, and grades them across five factors: Value, Growth, Profitability, Momentum, and EPS Revisions. The final quant rating is based on the collective investment fundamentals reflected in the five factor grades. Value, Growth, Profitability serve as fundamental indicators for identifying mispriced securities, while Momentum and EPS Revisions address timing - both often influenced by quarterly earnings results and other recent developments.

Netflix vs. Disney Stock: Momentum and Recent Earnings Results

Headquartered in Los Gatos, California, Netflix has soundly outperformed the market and sector in the past year for a ‘B-’ Momentum Grade, although shares plummeted after a huge Q3 earnings miss. Netflix Q3 GAAP EPS of $5.87 missed by $1.10, while revenue was in line at $11.51B (+17% YoY). The Q3 earnings miss was largely the result of expenses related to the company’s dispute with Brazilian tax authorities.

Revenue growth was primarily driven by increased membership and ad revenue, along with pricing adjustments. Netflix engagement remained healthy, reaching its highest quarterly view share ever in the US and UK – up 15% and 22%, respectively, since Q4 2022. Netflix noted long-term upside is huge considering they have captured only 8.6% of TV time in its largest market - the United States - and only 7% of consumer video spend.

Despite the long-term potential, Wall Street sell-side analysts have increasingly lowered their expectations. Netflix has 34 downward revisions in the last 90 days to only two upward revisions, driving a C- Revisions Grade.

In contrast to Netflix’s solid price performance, Disney’s stock has lagged in the past year, hit recently in a narrow top-line earnings miss and two-week blackout on YouTube TV over a contract dispute.

DTC was a major contributor to lifting FY25 revenue by 3% YoY to $94.43B, as Disney continues to build the streaming business. DTC revenue grew 8% to $24.61B - accounting for 26% of total company annual revenue - while the streaming segment’s operating income soared by $1.2B to $1.3B. Disney underscored the progress by noting DTC was running a $4B operating loss three years ago. Paid subscribers rose 5% to 131M on Disney+ and +23% to 64.1M on Hulu. Resorts and vacations revenue, along with theme park admissions drove solid growth in the Experiences segment.

Looking ahead, Disney is focused on accelerating growth in its streaming business, improving its app, and investing in global expansion, CEO Bob Iger said in the earnings call:

As we continue to build DTC into a core growth engine, we're rolling out a more unified app experience to better serve our consumers and unlock new value. In October, Hulu became our global general entertainment brand and we continue to work to consolidate all of our entertainment content domestically within a single app, which will simplify the user experience, highlight the full value of our bundles and unlock global marketing efforts. We're also expanding our international reach by investing strategically in our own originals and working with local studios to license content that brings more high-quality local storytelling to the platform. We're taking a disciplined approach to the markets we are prioritizing and we have confidence in our long-term strategy.”

For fiscal year 2026, Disney expects to deliver double-digit adjusted EPS growth and is targeting $7B in share repurchases, double the $3.5B repurchased in fiscal 2025. Wall Street analysts' optimism about Disney’s earnings forecast has grown – with 20 upward EPS revisions to four down revisions in the last 90 days.

The next section compares the strengths and weaknesses of core company fundamentals - profitability and growth. These are intrinsic qualities evaluated independently of market price.

Netflix vs. Disney Stock: Profitability and Growth

Both companies showcase A+ Profitability Grades, but Netflix significantly outperforms the sector in operating and free cash flow margins with its pure-play streaming model scaling efficiently. Disney has solid margins compared to the sector, although Netflix has an edge across the board in profitability. Disney differentiates itself with raw cash from operations of $18.10B.

Netflix has a strong edge in growth across the board, as the image below highlights, crushing the sector in revenue, EBIT, and long-term EPS forward growth rates. Netflix earnings are projected to grow more than 20% over the next three fiscal years, according to consensus estimates. Disney has an average growth grade, dragged down by slower growth in revenue and free cash flow, although performance in EPS, operating cash flow, and capex has been impressive. Earnings projections look solid for Disney, with EPS expected to rise by double digits in FY26 to FY28.

Netflix’s profitable growth and strong forecasts come at a hefty price relative to the sector. The next section examines how the stock’s valuation compares to its fundamentals.

Netflix vs. Disney Stock: Valuation

Based on most valuation multiples, Netflix’s strong long-term earnings growth upside comes at a steep price. Netflix is trading at over 40x earnings, a premium to the sector of more than 185%, while PEG forward of 1.70 represents a 22% premium, driving an ‘F’ Valuation Grade. Investors are also paying high premiums for Netflix’s robust revenue and operating profit based on EV/EBIT and price/sales multiples.

Disney’s Valuation Grade is a D+ – largely due to a weak dividend yield. Nonetheless, Disney is trading at a much more attractive price than Netflix according to most key valuation multiples. Disney’s P/E of 15.85x is only 7% higher than the sector median, while its PEG of 1.27 represents an 8.5% discount to communication peers.

Despite a 1.46% yield and semiannual payments, Disney offers a safe dividend with solid growth upside based on SA Quant Dividend Grades and consensus estimates for FY26 to FY28. Disney’s excellent dividend safety and growth grades are supported by low payout ratios, solid interest coverage, high operating leverage, and robust forward growth rates.

Netflix skyrocketed since its last stock split in 2015 and has delivered massive growth. Even with huge earnings growth projections and the stock falling sharply recently, Netflix is still trading at overstretched valuation multiples. Disney’s collective investment fundamentals underpin a Strong Buy Quant Rating, while Netflix’s failing valuation grade and declining momentum are keeping it at a Hold.

Conclusion: Disney Stock Offers Better Binging Than Netflix

Despite strong long-term earnings growth prospects, Netflix has sky-high valuation multiples and bearish earnings revision trends. Industry peer and competitor Disney looks like a better opportunity, showcasing a Strong Buy Quant Rating compared to Netflix’s Quant Hold rating. Disney possesses strong collective investment fundamentals, driven by robust profits and cash flow, and differentiates itself from Netflix by a much more attractive valuation and better EPS revisions.

We have many stocks with strong buy recommendations, and you can filter them using Stock Screens to suit your specific investment objectives. Alternatively, Alpha Picks might be ideal if you're interested in two monthly stock picks of the top 'strong buy' quant stocks. Seeking Alpha’s quant ratings and investment research tools help to ensure you are furnished with the best resources to make informed investment decisions while taking the emotion out of investing.

If you’re looking for more frequent investment ideas, we launched the PRO Quant Portfolio in June. It’s a weekly rebalanced selection of our Top 30 Quant Strong Buy stocks, spanning multiple regions and market caps. Happy Investing!

data.title: FEMSA Squeezed By Weaker Consumer Spending In Mexico
data.description:
Writing about Latin American retailer FEMSA (FMX) in March, I noted a discrepancy in the short-term and long-term investment outlooks for this well-run Mexican company and the likelihood and risk that weaker consumer conditions in Mexico could depress results in the short term.

So it has come to pass. Weak remittances and consumer confidence have weighed meaningfully on consumer spending in Mexico, and with the more discretionary nature of FEMSA’s OXXO C-store assortment, the company has had real struggles with same-store sales and earnings momentum. The shares, in turn, are basically flat since that last update, while comparables like Walmex (OTCQX:WMMVY) and Tiendas 3B (TBBB) have performed substantially better.

There are some legitimate challenges in front of FEMSA right now, including higher cash taxes, increasing competition from other small-format stores, and a need to fix the Mexican drugstore business. At the same time, this remains a well-run retailer and Coca-Cola bottler (through its Coca-Cola FEMSA (KOF) stake), and the growth potential for the company’s retail concepts throughout Latin America and in the U.S. is substantial. With easier comps ahead, I think this is a name that investors ought to consider again.

Weak OXXO Traffic Remains A Real Headwind

There’s no escaping the fact that FEMSA’s core OXXO operations in Mexico have not been performing well this year. Although it’s true that various consumer metrics (consumption, confidence, wages, et al.) have not been strong, the reality is that overall retail same-store sales (as reported by ANTAD) have continued to grow—with Q1 up 2.8%, Q2 up 5.8%, and Q3 up 3.5%.

Likewise, comparables like Walmex and TBBB have continued to grow, with 1.4% (Q1), 4.4% (Q2), and 3.9% (Q3) and 13.5%, 17.7%, and 17.9% same-store growth, respectively. Even granting that these aren’t ideal comps (they’re not c-stores, but there aren’t good direct comps, as Seven & i Holdings (OTCPK:SVNDY) and Alimentation Couche-Tard (OTCPK:ANCTF) do not report Mexico-specific information), they’re at least directionally relevant.

For its part, FEMSA has seen OXXO comps in Mexico down 2.6%, down 1.2%, and up 1.3% over the last three quarters, with traffic notably weak across the year.

Management isn’t ignoring the problem. The company has pushed affordability initiatives, working with tobacco companies (a high single-digit percentage of sales), beverage companies, and food companies to offer more affordable options, and has rejuvenated some bottle/packaging return programs, but the reality is that OXXO’s core business is more discretionary, and it doesn’t help that lower remittances are likely playing into lower traffic (with customers in the past going to the stores to get money transfers and pick up a few items while they’re there).

By no means do I think OXXO is “broken”—this isn’t the first time that the stores have seen a material impact from wider economic challenges in Mexico. I do think these affordability efforts will help improve same-store comps next year (as will easier comps), and I agree with management that there’s at least a decade’s worth of growth potential here, including new stores, the growth of the smaller-format OXXO Nichos concept, and further expansion of higher-margin businesses like financial services.

Multiple Growth Drivers, But A Few Other “To Do” Items As Well

Continuing on with the things that aren’t going so well for FEMSA, there are at least two other businesses that need some work at FEMSA—the Mexican drugstore business (part of FEMSA Health) and the Mexican gas station business (FEMSA Fuel).

Health Needs Some Intensive Care

While the Health business has been executing well in markets like Chile, the Mexican operations have been an issue for a long time now. Some of this can be attributed to the company’s decision not to invest more aggressively into expanding the business since the acquisition of Farmacias YZA more than a decade ago (there haven’t been large-scale M&A transactions, for instance).

The company still only has around 6% share of the Mexican market, limiting economies of scale, and the stores are overwhelmingly located in Sinaloa (an area with a noted crime problem). Likewise, the company hasn’t really been too active in trying to drive synergies between the Health and OXXO businesses in Mexico.

I don’t think this business is irretrievably broken, but a 12% yoy decline in same-store sales (after a 4% decline in the year-ago Q3) and the closure of over 400 stores over the past year are concerning. At this point, management likely needs to make a “stay or go” decision and either commit to spending the capital it will take to drive those synergies and make this a viable growth driver or decide to sell what’s left and move on.

Fuel Is Idling

While the Fuel operations haven’t been as disappointing as the Health operations in Mexico, they likewise haven’t done much for the business. By my calculations, same-store volumes of fuel sold have increased at an annualized rate of only 1.1% since 2019, and while the margins have definitely improved (with third-quarter 2025 gross, EBITDA, and operating margins of 11.8%, 6.5%, and 4.6%, respectively, compared to 10.0%, 4.5%, and 2.7% in Q3’19), they remain dilutive.

It's certainly possible that Fuel has contributed to improved traffic at OXXO and that this wouldn’t be explicitly evident in the numbers, but at best it’s a “meh” business. Perhaps there is more management could do to use Fuel as an “incentive” for increased shopping at OXXO (lower prices for Spin users, et al.), but for now it’s not a great use of capital.

Growth Drivers Remain Significant

With much of what is wrong (or at least challenging at present) at FEMSA out of the way, it’s worth remembering that there are material growth drivers here that can drive mid-to-high single-digit revenue growth across the next decade, as well as margin expansion and mid-teens free cash flow growth.

There is still ample room for more OXXO stores in Mexico, both standard-format and for the newer OXXO Nichos format. There is likewise ample room for more Bara, the company’s hard discount grocery chain. Management believes they can grow store count by 20%/year “for the foreseeable future,” and the same-store sales growth here has been solid (up 10.8% in Q3, up 8.9% in Q2, and 7.4% in Q1). While not growing at the same pace as TBBB, Bara is more proximity/convenience-focused, and there is room for improvement in areas like private label (about 20% of revenue now versus 50%+ at TBBB and a source of strength for OXXO).

There is also meaningful growth potential for OXXO outside of Mexico. The company amicably dissolved its joint venture with Raizen (co-owned by Cosan (CSAN)) earlier this year, and I believe that may have been at least partly due to FEMSA management wanting to pursue a faster pace of growth. Same-store growth has been strong over the last three quarters (22%, 12.8%, and 11.2%), and Brazil remains a large growth opportunity with only around 600 OXXO stores at present. Likewise, FEMSA is only just getting started in the U.S., and there are other Latin American countries where the company can introduce and grow the concept.

The Outlook

FEMSA certainly has taken some hits of late. Below-peer same-store sales at OXXO have been disappointing, and other challenges like higher cash taxes and higher labor costs won’t help. Likewise, EPS volatility has become a notable issue with the Street after some sizable net income/EPS misses driven by non-operating items, and that has led to an increased focus on P/E-based valuation vs. EV/EBITDA (and that’s not a favorable shift for FEMSA).

There’s also the risk of increased competition to consider. While Coca-Cola FEMSA is pretty much untouchable in the Mexican soft drink market, that’s not true everywhere, and many governments are actively looking to discourage consumption. Meanwhile, although OXXO maintains over 80% sales share in the Mexican C-store market, concepts like Six (owned by Heineken (OTCQX:HEINY)) and Modelorama (owned by Anhueser-Busch InBev (BUD)) have seen strong growth with their more beverage/snack-based format.

On balance, I think FEMSA’s growth drivers will win out over these challenges, but the new CEO has his work cut out to restore/rebuild investor confidence and trust over the next 12 months or so.

I am still bullish, but I’ve trimmed back my near-term revenue growth estimates in response to the weaker environment and weaker relative performance, and I’ve likewise pulled back a bit on near-term margins, even though the company has done comparatively well here. With all of that, I’m still looking for about 6% long-term annualized revenue growth and mid-teens FCF growth (closer to 14% now versus 16% before).

Discounting those cash flows back, I think the shares are undervalued below $100-$110 and priced for a double-digit long-term total annualized return. Multiples-based valuation is more challenging; historically a 7x forward EBITDA multiple has been pretty normal for FEMSA, and that would support a fair value of about $120, but with the Street now seemingly more focused on P/E, it’s harder to argue for undervaluation, as a peer-equivalent P/E of 17.5x (versus a historical range in the low-to-mid-20s) would only get me to around $80/share.

The Bottom Line

It’s early to sound an “all clear” on FEMSA shares, particularly as underwhelming same-store sales growth remains an issue. I do expect that management will get this fixed over the next year, and I’m likewise still very bullish on the long-term potential of the business and the stock. More patient investors should consider doing due diligence now, but with the understanding that it could still be another six to 12 months before these shares really work.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Why Canadian Stocks Have Been Quietly Outperforming Despite A 'Wall Of Worry'
data.description:
Canadian markets have spent the year climbing what some are calling a “never-ending wall of worry,” defying expectations at almost every turn. As 2026 approaches, Michael O’Brien, Managing Director and Portfolio Manager at TD Asset Management, looks at the big themes likely to drive markets, and potential opportunities and risks in the finance, insurance and resource sectors.

Transcript

Greg Bonnell: Canadian markets have spent the year climbing what many are calling a "never-ending wall of worry," defying expectations in almost every turn. You've got concerns about tariffs, politics, a fragile economy. Yet, the market has continued to push higher.

But with 2026 on the horizon, the questions are getting tougher and the stakes are getting higher. Joining us now to break it all down, Michael O'Brien, portfolio manager at TD Asset Management.

Michael, great to have you back on the program.

Michael O'Brien: Yeah, it's always a pleasure, Greg.

Greg Bonnell: You and I have had a lot of discussions this year about Canadian equities. We've been climbing this wall of worry. What was actually behind that resilience? Because in April, it didn't feel like this was a win situation for the TSX Composite Index.

Michael O'Brien: No, it really didn't feel that good back then, did it? So I think, two things that I would point to. First is, the simple fact that all these things we were worried about, it's not like they've disappeared into thin air. But they've been mitigated. They've been delayed. They've been watered down. They've been kicked down the road. So, none of those worst-case scenarios have actually unfolded. And sometimes not losing is a win. So I think that's been a part of it.

The second bigger part of it, though, I think is just that in response to this, policymakers have stepped up. And we look at that-- obviously, the Bank of Canada has done its part. We've gotten a couple of rate cuts this year, put a little monetary stimulus in, protect the economy. But the bigger sea change, obviously, has been on the political front and the fiscal front with the new liberal government in Ottawa and Prime Minister Carney and the nation-building projects.

And so I think, in one sense, there's the direct stimulative effect of these new projects that are being announced in a more stimulative fiscal stance. But also, I think it's a real-- been a real boon to confidence as well, and boosted people's optimism a little bit. So I think it's really that avoiding the worst-case outcomes, finding a way to muddle through, and then this newfound optimism about Canada that we haven't had for a while. I think that's really what's pushed the market up a little over 20% year to date.

Greg Bonnell: Right, so we're feeling a little bit better. That's playing through on the market. Some investors, though-- I mean, the headlines that are coming out are fast and furious. There's so many things to concentrate on. Forgive them, maybe-- we can forgive them if they say, "Oh, the tariffs? Political uncertainty? All that's behind us." You're still a little cautious, though.

Michael O'Brien: Oh, yeah. Well, I mean, this is not done. The first thing I would-- just as an example of this, just think back to the last few weeks, the skirmish between the US and China. We thought we had detente. We thought we had a trade deal a few months back.

All of a sudden, China is going to put restrictions on rare earth exports. The US is going to put restrictions on-- put Chinese companies on entity lists. Neither side liked that. They both thought they had an agreement. Clearly, they didn't. And so, there was some more brinksmanship.

Again, we've stepped back. It's been a de-escalation phase. But that's just an example of all these kind of deals that have been announced-- bilateral deals-- amongst so many trading partners in such a big part of the economy that I think some of these deals probably mean different things to different parties. And so I think there's lots of potential for misunderstandings or miscalculations, or just simply different opinions on what they actually agreed to.

The bigger one, though, for Canada specifically is, of all the stuff that has transpired, the thing that's still out in front of us is the biggest nut of all, which is the USMCA. And so, the USMCA agreement between Canada, US, Mexico-- critical to the Canadian economy-- that is up for its six-year renewal, or review. That has really just kicked off.

And so, this is going to be a story that's going to be with us well into the first half of 2026. And so, to me, that's the single biggest issue that Canada has in front of it, is, can we land the USMCA at some sort of a palatable arrangement where Canada's favored trading relationship with the US is preserved going forward?

Greg Bonnell: Is that your biggest concern for 2026? What else is on that list?

Michael O'Brien: That would be it.

Greg Bonnell: If that's number one, what else is on the list?

Michael O'Brien: Look, we could have a very long list. But if you want to keep it short, USMCA is the number one thing that I'm watching in the market today. I think if Canada can land a deal that provides an element of stability, provides an element of certainty, so that individuals, households, and especially the business community can begin making decisions, I think that'd be a huge win for the Canadian economy.

But as we've all seen, I mean, the administration south of the border is very mercurial. Goalposts tend to be moved quite a bit. And so, while I'm optimistic that there is a path to a USMCA agreement here, I'm by no means taking that for granted, because we can see that the carpet's been pulled out from underneath Canadian negotiators a number of times over the course of this year alone. So, I suspect there'll be a lot more twists and turns before this is done.

Greg Bonnell: And even when we think about the situation we have with the States right now because of the existing USMCA, there are tariffs, but a lot of it's exempt under that plan. Now we've got the Supreme Court. They've heard the case. Now they're weighing whether tariffs are even legal. That could be another bump in the road even before we hit 2026.

Michael O'Brien: Yeah, well, I mean, on the face of it you would think that would be good news if the tariffs are struck down. But my understanding is that there are a lot more tools in the toolkit. And this would be an inconvenience for the Trump administration. But I think, at the end of the day, they're still determined to wield tariffs in some form or another as a source of leverage, as a cudgel, as a bludgeoning instrument-- whatever you want to call it.

And so, I think there are other vehicles that they could use if these IEEPA tariffs are struck down. So, a court decision, it would probably feel good, maybe vindicate some of the feelings that we've had--

Greg Bonnell: You see that headline like, "Hey, we were right. We were right."

Michael O'Brien: Exactly. What was all that fentanyl stuff, anyway? But I don't think we should be under any illusion that the administration would stop at that. I think there will be other vehicles that will be used, similar to the sector tariffs that we've seen that are really impacting Canadian steel, automobiles, aluminum, lumber. I think that's-- we'll see more of those in lieu of the IEEPA tariffs, if that's the direction that the Supreme Court pushes them.

Greg Bonnell: The Bank of Canada, of course you said, delivered several rate cuts this year. They've indicated that they're on pause as far as the Fed is concerned. We're not sure what they're going to get up to in December. The market still thinks there's more to come. How important have those rate cuts been to propping up both economies?

Michael O'Brien: Well, I think we'll leave the economies for a second. Just in terms of the markets themselves, I think we've seen just in the last couple of weeks here, as soon as rumblings started coming out of the Fed and some of the FOMC speakers downplaying the potential for a December rate cut in the US, or even outright saying we're against a rate cut in December-- I think that was really what set off this latest market wobble. Whether it turns into a full-on correction, we'll find out over the next little while here.

So I think, for investor sentiment, the notion that the central banks have our backs is very important to keeping those good vibes going. I think in Canada they made it very clear they've done about as much as they can do for now, given some of the structural changes that are facing the economy, which monetary policy isn't well-suited for.

And also, just the simple fact that inflation here in Canada and in the States tends-- is still running at the high end of what we would used to consider a comfortable range, that 2 and 1/2, 3% range, that's not where monetary policy-- where policymakers want it. So I think, for now, the monetary card has largely been played.

In the US, I think there's an expectation there's more to come next year. But we'll see where that takes us. But in terms of the investor sentiment, it's been very important. In terms of the support to the economy itself, obviously monetary policy, it'll take 6, 9, 12 months to feed through. But it's a huge support for the economy, as well. So I would not dismiss that at all.

Greg Bonnell: Let's jump into some of the sectors. Now, I want to start with the banks. They have been performing with the market this year, perhaps. Are they one of the big performers in the market?

Michael O'Brien: The banks have had a very solid year. Yeah, they're handily outperforming the market by and large, up a little over 30% year to date. So, very solid results so far. As we're looking forward, though, I think the bar has been raised.

That 30%-plus return that we've seen from the banks this year, a decent portion of that was, again, better-than-expected outcomes. Their loan books have been more solid than maybe we would have expected at the beginning of the year. But also a lot of multiple appreciation, so that valuation rerate.

So, as we stand here today looking into 2026, the outlook is solid at this point in time, provided the Canadian economy holds up. But when valuations are in the 13-times forward earnings range, which is at the very high end of where banks traditionally trade, there isn't a lot of room for slip-ups on the earnings front.

So, that's the real challenge for the banks is, does the economy hold in there? Do the banks deliver the earnings that people expect? Because we're really walking that fine line here. There's not a lot of valuation cushion at the moment.

Greg Bonnell: Sounds like we need to go back to the federal budget on that one if we're talking about, can the economy hold up? The Bank of Canada has made clear it's done its part to support the economy-- monetary policy, a blunt tool. So now we have this budget. Is it about execution next year to make sure our economy grows-- and perhaps the banks can take part in that?

Michael O'Brien: I think there's two parts to it. The first part is the direct impact of what the fiscal measures are, which is trying to direct money to the economy. That pure, old-fashioned fiscal stimulus-- that is much needed. I would characterize the Canadian economy as fragile right now. So, I think we do need that fiscal shot in the arm.

The other big part of what the government is trying to do here, beyond just the direct impact of public money being put to work, is really trying to increase the confidence of households, but especially businesses, to begin investing and making decisions. And I think that's ultimately the biggest contribution that this Carney budget will have if it's successful, is really unfreezing this decision-making that I think has really handicapped the Canadian economy this year, and particularly on the business side.

If they can win over the corporate Canada and get them investing private dollars alongside the public incentives, I think that can have a real positive effect through 2025. So that's the bull case for Canada right there.

Greg Bonnell: The bull case there. And of course, if the economy does well, we look to the banks on that front. What about the insurers? What do they look like in this environment?
